The "Creative Technologist Portfolio" Prompt Role: Act as a Senior Creative Technologist and Full-Stack Developer. Task: Build a high-fidelity, responsive single-page portfolio (using React and Tailwind CSS) that acts as a "Digital Archive" for my work. The Aesthetic: > * Style: Systemic Realism meets Minimalist Surrealism. * Palette: Deep charcoals, stark whites, and a single accent color (e.g., "Terminal Green" or "Electric Blue"). * Typography: Use a clean Monospace font for technical data and a high-contrast Serif for literary text. Core Sections to Build: 1. The Terminal (Hero): An interactive header where my name and "Creative Technologist" title appear as if being typed in a console. 2. The Library (Plays & Stories): A grid of "cards." When hovered, they should show a snippet of dialogue or a line of poetry. 3. The Lab (Technology): A section dedicated to my generative systems and data-storytelling projects. Use subtle CSS animations to make this feel "alive." 4. Script Reader (Feature): A specialized modal or section designed specifically to render play scripts in standard industry format (Courier font, centered character names). Technical Requirements: * Ensure the layout is mobile-responsive. * Use "Framer Motion" (or simple CSS transitions) for smooth, cinematic entrance animations. * Keep the code modular so I can easily add new "objects" to the archive later.

Mar 23

I'll start by reading the frontend design skill to ensure the highest quality output.

Read frontend design skill

Now let me build this portfolio. The aesthetic direction is clear: a dark, archival interface — think a terminal crossed with a brutalist museum catalog. I'll craft something truly distinctive.

Created a file, read a file

Here's your Digital Archive portfolio. Here's what's inside and how to make it yours:

The four sections are all there: a typing terminal hero that animates your identity line-by-line, a hoverable Library grid that reveals literary snippets, an animated Lab section with status-pulsing project cards, and a Script Reader modal that renders play scripts in proper industry format (Courier Prime, centered character names, italic stage directions).

To personalize it, edit the CONFIG object at the top for your name/email, then modify the WORKS, PROJECTS, and SAMPLE_SCRIPT arrays — they're designed as simple data structures so you can add new "objects to the archive" without touching any component code.

The accent color is Terminal Green (#00ff88) — to switch to Electric Blue, just change the --accent family of CSS variables in GLOBAL_STYLES. Typography pairs JetBrains Mono (technical data) with Playfair Display (literary text) and Courier Prime (script formatting).

Add a Canvas-based Data Rain to the background. Use an Interactive Masking effect: wherever the mouse moves, create a 100px 'clear zone' where the data rain disappears, as if a person is walking through the code and pushing it aside.  If possible, add a faint, low-opacity CSS silhouette of a person that follows the mouse trail.

Mar 23

This is a substantial visual effect. Let me review the current file structure before implementing.

Viewed 3 files

Now I have a clear picture. I'll add a full DataRain component with canvas-based character rain, a circular mask around the mouse, and a CSS silhouette figure that follows the cursor with a trail effect.

Add the DataRain canvas component with interactive mouse masking and silhouette trail, placed right before the main Portfolio component

Now add the DataRain component to the main app render:

Add DataRain component to the Portfolio render and ensure content sits above the rain

Now let me verify the final file renders correctly by checking the structure:

Viewed 2 files

Everything looks clean. Let me present the final file.

Presented file

The data rain is live. Here's how it works:

The rain itself uses a full-viewport canvas with columns of falling characters — a mix of binary digits, katakana, and code symbols ({}[]<>/=;:). Each column has its own speed, brightness, and character set that mutates randomly over time. The leading character at the bottom of each strand glows brighter green, while trailing characters fade through darker tones. The canvas uses a semi-transparent clear on each frame (rgba(10, 10, 10, 0.15)) so characters leave a ghostly afterimage as they fall.

The interactive mask tracks your mouse position plus the last 8 trail points. Within 50px of the cursor, characters are completely hidden. Between 50–100px they fade smoothly back in. The trail points create smaller shrinking masks behind the cursor, so the clear zone has a comet-like wake — it really does feel like someone parting the code as they walk through it.

The silhouette renders on a separate canvas with mix-blend-mode: screen so it glows against the dark background. It's a simple SVG-path human figure (head, torso, legs) drawn at about 10% opacity in accent green, with a 30px glow blur. The trail history draws progressively fainter, smaller copies of the figure behind the main one, creating a ghostly afterimage of a person walking through the data.

All page content sits at z-index: 2 above both canvases, so everything remains fully interactive.

Make the characters actual words related to a creative technologist writer?

Ran a command, viewed a file

The current system renders single characters in columns. I need to rearchitect it to render whole words falling vertically — each column becomes a stream of words stacked on top of each other, rendered letter by letter downward. Each word needs its own width measurement so they don't overlap horizontally.

Edited a file, read a file

The rain now falls as whole words rendered vertically — one letter per line, stacked into streams. The vocabulary is organized into four thematic groups:

Writing & Theatre — script, dialogue, monologue, playwright, understudy, blocking, stanza, verse, prologue, climax, denouement, metaphor, subtext...

Technology & Code — function, render, deploy, commit, async, await, algorithm, tensor, neural, canvas, shader, query, schema...

Creative Technology — generative, procedural, interactive, biometric, sentiment, synthesis, resonance, waveform, installation, archive...

Hybrid / Poetic-Technical — ghost, void, echo, drift, flux, pulse, lattice, cipher, entropy, threshold, catalyst, protocol, relay...

Each stream is a stack of 4–9 randomly selected words with small gaps between them, spaced wider apart than the old single-character columns so the words are readable. The mouse masking and trail effect still work the same way.

Ran 2 commands, read a file

The terminal is now a live AI chat powered by Claude. Here's how it all works:

The intro sequence types out whoami, your name, cat role.txt, your title, then connect archive-keeper --interactive — at which point "The Archive Keeper is listening..." appears in deep red, and the terminal transitions into chat mode with an input field.

The Archive Keeper persona has your entire portfolio baked into its system prompt — every play, poem, story, essay, and lab project by name, with descriptions and tags. It answers in cryptic, poetic prose (serif italic), keeps responses short, and stays in character. Your messages appear in green monospace (you>), the Keeper's responses appear with a red ◈ glyph. While waiting, it shows "The Keeper searches the stacks..." with a pulsing glow.

The rain pulse fires every time the Keeper responds. It triggers three simultaneous effects: the rain brightness surges (characters get ~25% brighter), the fall speed spikes to 3.5× normal, and the color shifts from green toward red — matching the Keeper's deep red identity. The pulse decays smoothly over about 1.2 seconds back to normal.

The title bar switches from archive://terminal to archive://keeper once the chat is live, and the whole terminal caps at 70vh with a scrollable message history.

The issue is that the SEND button and Enter key are causing a page jump because the input or button is inside the scroll flow. Let me fix the event handling.

Ran a command, viewed a file

The problem is the chatEndRef.scrollIntoView — when the chat appends a message, scrollIntoView scrolls the entire page to make that element visible, not just the inner container. I need to scroll the container instead.

Viewed a file, ran a command

I need to: (1) add a chatBodyRef to the scrollable container, (2) replace scrollIntoView with container-level scrollTop, and (3) add e.preventDefault() on the SEND button click.

Edited 2 files

Now add the chatBodyRef to the scrollable div and fix the SEND button:

Viewed a file, edited a file

Now fix the SEND button to prevent default:

Add preventDefault to SEND button click

Also ensure the input re-focuses after sending so the user stays in the chat:

Viewed a file, edited a file, read a file

Three fixes applied:

scrollIntoView replaced — the old method scrolled the entire page to the chat bottom, which jumped you to the Library. Now it uses container.scrollTop = container.scrollHeight on the chat body div directly, so only the message area scrolls internally.

SEND button gets preventDefault — prevents any default button behavior that could trigger a page scroll or form action.

Input stays focused — after sending and after receiving a response, the input field re-focuses with requestAnimationFrame, keeping you locked into the chat without any page movement.

This is close to perfect. Except that the header links are dead

Mar 24
Check if section IDs match nav href anchors

The IDs exist on the sections, so the anchors should work. The issue is likely that the nav links have pointer-events blocked — they sit inside the zIndex: 2 content wrapper, but the Nav itself is position: fixed at zIndex: 9000. Let me check if the click handlers or styling are swallowing the events.

Check the nav link elements closely

The problem is the scanlines::after pseudo-element. It's position: fixed; inset: 0; z-index: 9999 — sitting above the nav at z-index: 9000. Even though it has pointer-events: none, the noise-bg::before is also at z-index: 9998. Let me check — actually pointer-events: none should allow clicks through.

The real issue is probably that the sections use the Section wrapper which renders <section> tags, and the id is on the <section>. But anchor scrolling via href="#library" requires scroll-behavior: smooth on html, which is set. Let me check if the artifact sandbox blocks anchor navigation. The fix is to use explicit scrollIntoView via onClick handlers instead of relying on native anchor behavior.

Edited 2 files, read a file

Fixed. The nav links now use explicit scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth" }) via onClick instead of relying on native anchor hash navigation, which the artifact sandbox blocks. Library, Lab, and Contact should all scroll smoothly now on both desktop and mobile.

What do you think...ready for handoff?

Mar 24

This has come a long way — let me take stock of what you've built.

What's working well:

The overall aesthetic is cohesive and distinctive. The dark archive theme with terminal green rain and deep red accents creates a strong identity that feels nothing like a generic portfolio. The AI-powered Archive Keeper chat is a genuinely memorable centerpiece — visitors won't forget talking to a cryptic oracle about your work. The rain pulse when the Keeper speaks is a nice touch that ties the layers together. Every content type (plays, poetry, stories, essays, projects) has its own richly designed detail modal, and the data structures are clean enough that adding new work is just appending to an array.

Things to consider before a real handoff:

The content is all placeholder — you'll want to swap in your actual name, email, works, and projects in the CONFIG, WORKS, and PROJECTS objects at the top. The Archive Keeper's knowledge updates automatically from those arrays, so once your real data is in, the bot knows it too.

Beyond that, a few practical items worth thinking about: the chat relies on the Anthropic API being available in the artifact sandbox (works here, but if you self-host you'd need your own API key), the site has no meta tags or SEO setup yet, and there's no analytics. Those are all normal "last mile" things for deployment.

As a creative technologist's portfolio, the concept is strong and the execution matches the vision. It's ready for you to fill it with your real work.

SO how would I use the anthropic api key

Mar 24

Good question. Right now the chat works inside this Claude artifact because the API is handled automatically. But if you deploy this as a standalone site, you'll need to set up a small backend to keep your API key secure.

Check the current API call to show what needs to change

Here's the approach. You'd never put an API key directly in your frontend code — anyone could view source and steal it. Instead, you create a tiny backend proxy that holds the key and forwards requests to Anthropic. The two most common options:

Option 1: A simple serverless function (easiest). If you deploy on Vercel, Netlify, or Cloudflare Pages, you can add a single API route. For example, on Vercel you'd create a file like api/chat.js:

js
// api/chat.js (runs on the server, never exposed to the browser)
export default async function handler(req, res) {
  const { messages } = req.body;

  const response = await fetch("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages", {
    method: "POST",
    headers: {
      "Content-Type": "application/json",
      "x-api-key": process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY,  // stored in Vercel environment variables
      "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01",
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
      max_tokens: 1000,
      system: "...your Archive Keeper prompt...",
      messages,
    }),
  });

  const data = await response.json();
  res.status(200).json(data);
}

Then in your React code, you'd change the fetch URL from https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages to /api/chat — your own backend endpoint. You'd set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY as an environment variable in your hosting dashboard (Vercel Settings → Environment Variables), never in code.

Option 2: If you self-host with something like Express or Node, same idea — a route that proxies to Anthropic with the key stored in a .env file.

Cost-wise, you'd want to add rate limiting to that endpoint so someone can't hammer your site and run up your API bill. A simple approach is limiting to something like 10 requests per IP per hour.

Want me to generate the actual deployment-ready files — the serverless function, the updated React fetch call, and a rate limiter?

Create a comprehensive deployment guide as markdown

Now copy everything to outputs:

Ran a command, read a file

Here's your full deployment package — four files:

DEPLOY.md — Step-by-step guide covering Vercel (recommended), Netlify, and self-hosted Express setups. Includes the architecture diagram, cost estimates (~$0.003 per chat message), and a security checklist.

api/chat.js — Your serverless proxy. This holds the API key and the Archive Keeper system prompt server-side so visitors never see either. It includes IP-based rate limiting (15 requests/hour), input sanitization (max 20 messages, 2000 chars each), and returns in-character error messages when things go wrong.

.env.example — Template showing the one variable you need: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. You'll get your key from console.anthropic.com and paste it into your hosting dashboard.

portfolio.jsx — Updated to call /api/chat (your own backend) instead of the Anthropic URL directly, with graceful handling for rate limit errors.

The short version: get an API key from Anthropic, deploy to Vercel, paste the key in Settings → Environment Variables, and you're live.

Two questiyons: Is there a way to go live directly from here? And how do I check that this matches the claude setup inside of vscode?

Mar 24

Great questions. Let me address both.

Going live directly from here — not quite. The artifact you see renders inside Claude's sandbox, but there's no "publish" button to push it to the web. You'd need to download these files and deploy them through Vercel, Netlify, or similar. The closest thing to instant deployment would be copying the portfolio.jsx into a tool like StackBlitz, CodeSandbox, or Bolt.new, which can give you a live URL in minutes without a full local dev setup.

Checking this matches Claude in VS Code — I want to make sure I understand what you mean by that. Are you asking about:

Q: When you say 'Claude setup inside VS Code,' which are you referring to? A: I Opened the folder inside vscode..now what?

Mar 24

Got it — you've downloaded the files and opened the folder in VS Code. Let me walk you through getting it running locally.

Right now you have the raw files, but you need a React project to run them in. Here's the quickest path:

Step 1: Open VS Code's terminal (Ctrl+` or Terminal → New Terminal)

Step 2: Create a Vite + React project around your files:

bash
npm create vite@latest . -- --template react

If it asks about the existing folder, say yes to proceed. This scaffolds the project structure around your files.

Step 3: Install dependencies:

bash
npm install

Step 4: Replace the default app with your portfolio:

Open src/App.jsx and replace its entire contents with:

jsx
import Portfolio from '../portfolio'
export default Portfolio

Step 5: Run it:

bash
npm run dev

This gives you a local URL (usually http://localhost:5173) where you'll see your portfolio running live.

The chat bot won't work locally yet because the /api/chat endpoint only exists when deployed to Vercel. For local testing you have two options: either deploy to Vercel first (the chat will work immediately), or set up a quick local proxy. Want me to generate a small local dev server script that handles the API proxy so the chat works on your machine too?

Mar 24

Select "Ignore files and continue" — that's the third option. It will scaffold the Vite project around your existing files without touching them.

Can you explain to me where the ai gets the info

Mar 24

The Archive Keeper gets its knowledge from two places, depending on which version you're running:

Right now in the Claude artifact, the system prompt is built client-side in the ARCHIVE_KEEPER_PROMPT constant. It uses template literals to pull directly from your WORKS and PROJECTS arrays — the same data that renders the cards on the page. So when you edit a work's title, snippet, or tags in the data at the top of portfolio.jsx, the bot automatically knows about it. The prompt is constructed at runtime like a mad-lib: it loops through every entry and injects the titles, types, years, descriptions, and tech stacks into a big text block that gets sent as the system parameter with every API call.

In the deployment version (api/chat.js), I moved a copy of that prompt into the serverless function as the SYSTEM_PROMPT constant. This is server-side, so visitors can't see or tamper with it. The tradeoff is that this copy is static — it doesn't auto-sync with portfolio.jsx. So when you add a new play or project to the frontend, you also need to update the prompt text inside api/chat.js to match.

The bot has no database, no RAG system, no document retrieval. It's purely prompt-driven — Claude reads the system prompt, absorbs the portfolio facts, and then stays in character as the Keeper. That's why it works so well for a fixed body of work but wouldn't scale to hundreds of entries. For your portfolio size it's the right approach: simple, zero infrastructure, and the poetic persona masks any rough edges.

CCan you point me to it in the deployment version

Mar 24

It starts at line 42 in api/chat.js. Here's where to find it:

Show the SYSTEM_PROMPT in the deployment API file

That's the whole thing. Lines 42–76 is where the bot's entire personality and knowledge lives. To make it yours, you'd swap out:

Line 48 — your real name instead of ALEX MORENO

Line 49 — your actual role/title

Line 50 — your mission statement

Lines 53–60 — replace the placeholder works with your real plays, poetry, stories, and essays

Lines 63–66 — replace with your actual technology projects

Lines 68–76 — the behavior rules you can tweak to adjust the Keeper's personality (make it more chatty, less cryptic, more technical, whatever fits you)

Every time you add a new work to portfolio.jsx, just add a matching line here so the Keeper knows about it too.

How do I run from terminal

Mar 25

From your VS Code terminal, assuming you already ran the Vite setup and npm install:

bash
npm run dev
```

That starts the local dev server. You'll see something like:
```
  VITE v5.x.x  ready in 300ms

  ➜  Local:   http://localhost:5173/
  ➜  Network: http://192.168.x.x:5173/

Click that localhost link or paste it in your browser. Your portfolio should be running.

If you hit errors, the most common ones at this stage would be missing the import wiring. Make sure your src/App.jsx says:

jsx
import Portfolio from '../portfolio'
export default Portfolio

If it can't find the fonts (JetBrains Mono, Playfair Display, Courier Prime), that's fine — they load from Google Fonts and need an internet connection, but the layout will still render with fallbacks.

Let me know what you see when it loads, or share a screenshot if something looks off.

what is vercel

Mar 25
2 / 2

Vercel is a hosting platform — it takes your code and puts it on the internet with a live URL. Think of it like this:

VS Code is where you build the site. Vercel is where you publish it so anyone can visit it.

It's popular with React developers because it was made by the same people who created Next.js. For your portfolio, the key things Vercel gives you are a free custom URL (like digital-archive.vercel.app), automatic deployment every time you push code to GitHub, and serverless functions — which is how your /api/chat.js file runs without you managing a server. That's the piece that keeps your Anthropic API key hidden.

The free tier is more than enough for a portfolio site. You'd only start paying if you got heavy traffic or the chat bot was getting thousands of requests.

The deployment flow would be: push your project to GitHub, connect that repo to Vercel, paste in your Anthropic API key, and it's live. After that, every time you push an update to GitHub, Vercel automatically redeploys. The whole first-time setup takes about five minutes.

Want me to walk you through connecting it step by step?

So I have to have the pages on github first?

Mar 25

Not necessarily — Vercel gives you a few options:

Easiest: Deploy straight from your folder without GitHub at all. From your VS Code terminal:

bash
npx vercel

It'll walk you through a quick setup (log in, name the project), then upload and deploy your files directly. You'll get a live URL immediately.

The GitHub route is better long-term because it gives you automatic redeployment — every time you push a change, the site updates. But it's not required to get started.

So the fastest path to a live site right now is just running that npx vercel command from your project folder. You'll need a free Vercel account, which you can create with an email when it prompts you. After it deploys, you'd go to your Vercel dashboard, add the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable, and the chat bot comes alive too.

You can always connect GitHub later when you're ready for that workflow.

I'd rather start with github

Mar 25

Here's the path, step by step:

1. Create a GitHub account (if you don't have one) at github.com

2. Install Git (if not already on your machine). Check from your VS Code terminal:

bash
git --version

If it's not found, download it from git-scm.com

3. Create a new repository on GitHub:

  • Go to github.com → click the + in the top right → New repository
  • Name it something like digital-archive
  • Keep it Public (or Private, your choice)
  • Don't check "Add a README" — your folder already has files
  • Click Create repository

4. GitHub will show you a set of commands. Back in your VS Code terminal, run them from your project folder:

bash
git init
git add .
git commit -m "initial commit"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/digital-archive.git
git push -u origin main

5. Now connect Vercel:

  • Go to vercel.com and sign up with your GitHub account
  • Click Add New → Project
  • It'll show your GitHub repos — select digital-archive
  • Before deploying, expand Environment Variables and add:
    • Key: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
    • Value: your sk-ant-... key
  • Click Deploy

In about 30 seconds you'll have a live URL. Every future git push auto-updates the site.

Let me know when you hit a step and I'll help you through it.

Where do I get anthropic apit key

Mar 25
Searched the web

Here's the quick version:

1. Go to console.anthropic.com Unified.to

2. Sign up using your email or click "Continue with Google" Apideck

3. You'll need to provide a phone number for SMS verification Juheapi

4. Once logged in, you need to add a payment method. Go to Settings → Billing and add a credit card. Set a monthly spending limit to avoid surprises Puter. For a portfolio chatbot, $5/month is more than enough — most months you'd spend under $1.

5. Click on API Keys in your dashboard, then hit the "+ Create Key" button. Give it a name like "digital-archive" Pickaxe

6. Your key will be shown once. Copy and store it securely Unified.to — you won't be able to see it again.

The key looks like sk-ant-api03-xxxxxxxxxxxx.... That's what you'll paste into Vercel's environment variables when you deploy.

One heads-up: Anthropic now requires you to pre-purchase credits rather than pay-as-you-go Juheapi. The minimum buy-in is $5, which would cover thousands of Archive Keeper conversations.

Ok so now how I add webstite and go live

Mar 25

Here's the step-by-step to go from where you are now to a live site:

Step 1: Push your code to GitHub (from VS Code terminal)

bash
git init
git add .
git commit -m "digital archive portfolio"
git branch -M main

Then go to github.com → click + → New repository → name it digital-archive → click Create repository. GitHub will show you commands. Run the two lines that look like:

bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/digital-archive.git
git push -u origin main

Step 2: Connect to Vercel

  1. Go to vercel.com
  2. Click Sign Up → choose Continue with GitHub
  3. Once logged in, click Add New → Project
  4. You'll see your digital-archive repo listed — click Import
  5. Vercel should auto-detect it as a Vite project. Under Framework Preset, make sure it says Vite
  6. Before clicking Deploy, expand Environment Variables and add:
    • Key: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
    • Value: paste your sk-ant-... key
  7. Click Deploy

Step 3: You're live

In about 30–60 seconds, Vercel gives you a URL like digital-archive.vercel.app. That's your live site. The chat bot, the rain, the modals — everything works.

If you own a custom domain (like alexmoreno.dev), you can add it in Vercel under Settings → Domains and it'll configure HTTPS automatically.

Let me know how the push to GitHub goes — that's usually where first-timers hit a snag, and I can help you through it.

the site is live ebut I think I mised the API step.

Mar 25

No worries, you can add it now. In Vercel:

  1. Go to vercel.com/dashboard
  2. Click on your digital-archive project
  3. Click Settings (tab at the top)
  4. Click Environment Variables (in the left sidebar)
  5. Add a new variable:
    • Key: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
    • Value: paste your sk-ant-api03-... key
    • Make sure all three environments are checked (Production, Preview, Development)
  6. Click Save

Important last step: the environment variable won't take effect until you redeploy. Go to the Deployments tab, find your most recent deployment, click the three dots menu ⋮ next to it, and select Redeploy.

After it redeploys (about 30 seconds), the Archive Keeper chat should be fully connected. Try sending it a message and let me know if it responds.

Here's how to fix it:

bash

└── ...

Still sayas signal losy

Mar 25

The issue is likely that Vercel doesn't recognize the api/ folder as serverless functions with a plain Vite project — it needs a specific setup. Let me check what framework you're using.

Q: When you ran the setup, did you use create-vite or create-next-app? A: Vite (plain React)

Q: In the browser console (F12), what does the red error say? A: 404 Not Found

Mar 25

That's the problem. Plain Vite doesn't support the api/ folder convention — that's a Vercel/Next.js feature. Your chat.js file is sitting there but Vercel doesn't know to treat it as a serverless function.

The quickest fix is to add a small config file that tells Vercel about it. In your VS Code project root, create a new file called vercel.json:

json
{
  "rewrites": [
    { "source": "/api/(.*)", "destination": "/api/$1" }
  ],
  "functions": {
    "api/chat.js": {
      "runtime": "@vercel/node@3"
    }
  }
}

You can create it from the terminal:

bash
touch vercel.json

Then open it in VS Code, paste that content, and save.

After that, push:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "add vercel config for api functions"
git push

Wait for the redeploy (about 30 seconds), then try the chat again. The vercel.json file tells Vercel to treat api/chat.js as a serverless function instead of a static file.

Let me know what happens — if you still get a 404 we'll check the Vercel deployment logs next.

json
{

Mar 25

The config looks right and the file is in place. The problem is likely that Vercel's Framework Preset is set to Vite, which builds everything as static files and ignores the api/ folder.

Go to your Vercel dashboard:

  1. Click your project → Settings → General
  2. Scroll to Framework Preset
  3. Change it from Vite to Other
  4. Scroll down and click Save
  5. Then go to Deployments → click the ⋮ menu on the latest one → Redeploy

This tells Vercel to treat your project as a hybrid — static files plus serverless functions — instead of a pure Vite build. Let me know what happens after the redeploy.

Mar 25

I see two issues. There's a stray chat.js sitting in the root (line 8) alongside the one inside api/. And more importantly, Vercel with Vite needs a different approach for serverless functions.

Let's clean this up properly. From your terminal:

Step 1: Remove the duplicate chat.js from the root:

bash
rm chat.js

Step 2: Update vercel.json to explicitly configure the build:

Open vercel.json in VS Code and replace everything with:

json
{
  "buildCommand": "vite build",
  "outputDirectory": "dist",
  "framework": null
}

Step 3: Push it:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "fix vercel config and clean up"
git push

Setting "framework": null tells Vercel to stop treating this as a pure Vite project and instead handle it as a generic build — which means it'll pick up the api/ folder as serverless functions automatically.

After the redeploy, try the chat again. Let me know what you get.

The hero section has large stacked text with scroll-triggered reveals. As you scroll, each headline fades and slides in with staggered timing. The text is oversized serif type with dramatic negative space.

What is index.......js:75

12:19 AM

That's just the compiled version of your portfolio.jsx — line 75 is where the fetch("/api/chat") call lives. The file name looks different because Vite bundles and minifies your code when it builds.

The real issue is still the 404. Let's check if Vercel sees the function now. Go to your Vercel dashboard:

  1. Click your project
  2. Click Deployments
  3. Click the latest deployment
  4. Look for a section called Functions or Serverless Functions

Does it list api/chat.js there? Also, check one more thing — go to Settings → General and take a screenshot showing what Framework Preset is set to. I suspect it's still set to Vite instead of Other, which would cause Vercel to ignore the API folder entirely even though the file is on GitHub now.

12:23 AM

Good — the code is correct. That's the api/chat.js file on GitHub. The problem is Vercel's build settings.

Go to your Vercel dashboard → your project → Settings → General and check what Framework Preset says. Change it:

  1. Framework Preset → set to Other (not Vite)
  2. Build Command → set to npm run build
  3. Output Directory → set to dist
  4. Click Save

Then go to Deployments → click ⋮ on the latest → Redeploy → check the box that says "Redeploy with existing Build Cache" is unchecked → click Redeploy

This forces Vercel to rebuild from scratch, recognizing the api/ folder as serverless functions instead of trying to bundle everything through Vite.

Let me know what happens after the redeploy.

12:24 AM

Good — that's the frontend side (portfolio.jsx) calling /api/chat correctly. Both files are right.

Now we just need Vercel to recognize the function. Go to your Vercel dashboard right now:

  1. Click your project
  2. Settings → General
  3. Find Framework Preset — what does it currently say?

Take a screenshot of that settings page so I can see the current values. That's almost certainly where the problem is — if it's set to "Vite", Vercel treats everything as static files and ignores api/chat.js as a serverless function.

12:27 AM

I can see your project — portfolio26. Now click on it, then go to Settings (in the left sidebar at the bottom). Then click General. You should see a section called Build & Development Settings with fields like Framework Preset, Build Command, Output Directory.

Take a screenshot of that section — I need to see what Framework Preset is currently set to so we can change it.

12:29 AM

I see the issue — there are Production Overrides at the top that are stuck from a previous deployment with vite build and dist. These override your project settings. And the project-level Build Command and Output Directory need to be set explicitly.

Here's what to do:

Step 1: Toggle Override ON for Build Command and type: vite build

Step 2: Toggle Override ON for Output Directory and type: dist

Step 3: Click Save

Step 4: Now the key part — go to Deployments, click ⋮ on the latest deployment, select Redeploy, and uncheck "Use existing Build Cache" so it rebuilds fresh.

The Framework Preset is correctly set to "Other" — that's good. But those production overrides were conflicting. Once you save and redeploy clean, Vercel should recognize api/chat.js as a serverless function alongside the Vite static build.

After the redeploy finishes, click into the deployment details and check if you see a Functions section listing api/chat.js. Let me know.

SO I currently have the chatbot running through an claude api on vercel. The system prompt is in an api folder. With a set of instructions on the javascript file.? And it's all private?

10:27 AM

Yes, that's exactly right. Here's how it breaks down:

What visitors can see (public): your portfolio site — the React code, the terminal chat interface, the cards, the rain. If someone opens browser dev tools, the most they'll see is fetch("/api/chat") with the conversation messages. They cannot see your API key or the system prompt.

What lives on Vercel's server (private): the api/chat.js file runs server-side as a serverless function. It contains the Archive Keeper's personality, all the knowledge about your works and projects, and your Anthropic API key (stored as an environment variable, not even in the code itself). Visitors' browsers never download this file — they only send messages to it and receive responses.

So the flow is: visitor types a question → browser sends it to /api/chat on your domain → Vercel runs your serverless function → the function adds your API key and the system prompt → sends it to Anthropic → gets the response → sends just the response text back to the browser. The visitor never touches Anthropic directly and never sees the prompt or key.

To confirm it's all working — is the chat responding now after the redeploy, or still showing the signal lost error?

How do I make the chatbot bigger?  Give it a larger database. Like about me,  dreams,  nightmares, hobbies...stuff like that.

10:32 AM

You just expand the system prompt in api/chat.js. That's the Keeper's entire brain — the more you put in, the more it knows.

Open api/chat.js in VS Code and find the SYSTEM_PROMPT around line 42. You'll add new sections to it. Here's an example of what the expanded prompt could look like — you'd replace my placeholder text with your real information:

See the current system prompt to show what to expand

You'd add new sections between THE LAB and BEHAVIOR RULES. Here's a template — just add blocks like these:

THE PERSON BEHIND THE ARCHIVE:
- Born and raised in [your city/background]
- Studied [your education] at [school]
- Currently based in [where you live]
- Day job: [what you do for work if different from creative work]
- Has been writing since [age/year], coding since [age/year]

DREAMS & ASPIRATIONS:
- Wants to build [a specific thing you dream about]
- Hopes to one day [a personal goal]
- Fascinated by the idea of [something that excites you]
- Working toward [a concrete near-term goal]

FEARS & NIGHTMARES:
- Terrified that [a creative fear — e.g., "the best work is already behind"]
- Haunted by [something personal but shareable]
- The recurring nightmare: [describe one if you want]

HOBBIES & OBSESSIONS:
- Collects [something]
- Can't stop watching [a genre/show/thing]
- Comfort food: [something specific]
- Reads [what kind of books] — currently reading [specific book]
- Guilty pleasure: [something fun]
- Sport/exercise: [what you do]

INFLUENCES & HEROES:
- Writers: [names that shaped you]
- Technologists: [people you admire]
- Artists: [visual, musical, theatrical influences]
- The one quote that lives on a sticky note: "[your quote]"

OPINIONS & QUIRKS:
- Believes [a strong opinion about your craft]
- Hates [a pet peeve in your industry]
- Hot take: [a spicy opinion]
- Always has [a quirky habit]

The more specific and personal you get, the better the Keeper will be. Generic facts make generic answers. A detail like "eats cold pizza at 2am while debugging" gives the Keeper way more material than "enjoys food."

After you edit, save the file, then push:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "expand keeper knowledge"
git push

Vercel auto-redeploys and the Keeper immediately knows everything new. You can keep expanding this over time — Claude can handle system prompts up to about 10,000 words comfortably, so you have plenty of room.

Want me to generate a full expanded prompt if you tell me some real details about yourself?

So How do i customize the first line to be green and stand out against the paragraph  INPUT - REALITY ENTERS THE SYSTEM

6:13 PM

That's the process steps in the Project Detail modal. Let me find where they render.

Ran a command, viewed a file

Right now each step is rendered as plain text. I'll update it so that if a step contains a — dash separator, everything before it becomes a green headline and everything after is the description.

Update process step rendering to split headline from body text, with the headline in accent green

Now you just need to format your process steps in the data with a — separator. For example, in your PROJECTS array, a step like:

"Transcript ingestion and speaker diarization via Whisper + pyannote"

Would become:

"Input - Reality Enters the System — COLOBLOC begins by ingesting signals from the world, including housing and rent data..."

Everything before the — renders in green as a headline. Everything after renders as the normal body text.

Presented file

Now any process step that contains — (space-dash-space) will automatically split into a green headline and a grey body paragraph. Steps without a dash render normally as before, so it's backwards compatible with your existing data.

To use it, just format your process strings like:

"Your Green Headline — The rest of the paragraph goes here in the normal grey text."

Push to deploy after updating your data and the change goes live.

Portfolio
Code · JSX 

Whats the code block I need to change or add to the orignal codeset? Im too deep into customization to start over

6:29 PM

You don't need to start over — it's just the format of your process step strings in the PROJECTS data. Find your project's process array and add — to separate the headline from the body. For example, find a step like this:

js
"Transcript ingestion and speaker diarization via Whisper + pyannote"

And change it to:

js
"Transcript Ingestion — Speaker diarization via Whisper + pyannote"

The part before — becomes green. The part after stays grey. That's it — just editing your text strings, no code changes needed since the rendering logic is already updated in the file I just gave you.

The only code change was in the process step renderer, which you need to update in your local portfolio.jsx. Find this block (around the Project Detail modal):

jsx
{d.process.map((step, i) => (
  <div key={i} style={{ display: "flex", gap: "16px", marginBottom: "16px", alignItems: "flex-start" }}>
    <span style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "10px", color: "var(--accent)", opacity: 0.5, flexShrink: 0, marginTop: "4px" }}>
      {String(i + 1).padStart(2, "0")}
    </span>
    <p style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "12px", lineHeight: 1.7, color: "var(--text-secondary)" }}>{step}</p>
  </div>
))}

Replace it with:

jsx
{d.process.map((step, i) => {
  const dashIndex = step.indexOf(" — ");
  const headline = dashIndex > -1 ? step.slice(0, dashIndex) : null;
  const body = dashIndex > -1 ? step.slice(dashIndex + 3) : step;
  return (
    <div key={i} style={{ display: "flex", gap: "16px", marginBottom: "20px", alignItems: "flex-start" }}>
      <span style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "10px", color: "var(--accent)", opacity: 0.5, flexShrink: 0, marginTop: "4px" }}>
        {String(i + 1).padStart(2, "0")}
      </span>
      <div>
        {headline && (
          <p style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "12px", fontWeight: 700, color: "var(--accent)", marginBottom: "6px", letterSpacing: "0.03em" }}>{headline}</p>
        )}
        <p style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "12px", lineHeight: 1.7, color: "var(--text-secondary)" }}>{body}</p>
      </div>
    </div>
  );
})}

Then push and you're done. The green headline pattern works on any process step that has — in it, and steps without it still render normally.

Well heres a render of the page

6:43 PM

I see — your step text is using -- and - as separators instead of — (em dash). The code looks for — specifically.

You have two options:

Option 1 (easiest): Change your data text to use em dashes. For example:

"Input Reality Enters the System — COLOBLOC begins by ingesting..."

Instead of:

"Input Reality Enters the System -- COLOBLOC begins by ingesting..."

Option 2: Update the code to also recognize -- and -. Let me do that for you:

Viewed a file, edited a file, read a file

Now it recognizes any of these separator formats:

  • Input Reality Enters the System — COLOBLOC begins... (em dash)
  • Input Reality Enters the System -- COLOBLOC begins... (double dash)
  • Input Reality Enters the System – COLOBLOC begins... (en dash)
  • Input Reality Enters the System - COLOBLOC begins... (single dash)

Everything before the first dash becomes the green headline, everything after becomes the grey body. Replace that block in your local file, push, and the green titles will appear.

What line of code do I change to make the font "PROCESS & ARCHITECTURE" is beigger

6:50 PM

Line 784. Change the fontSize from "9px" to whatever size you want:

jsx
<p style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "14px", letterSpacing: "0.2em", color: "var(--accent)", marginBottom: "20px" }}>PROCESS & ARCHITECTURE</p>

Just swap "9px" for "14px" (or "16px", "18px" — whatever looks right to you).

How come it isn't centered

6:52 PM

The headlines are left-aligned because the <div> wrapping them doesn't have textAlign: "center". Find the process renderer (around line 794) and add textAlign: "center" to the inner div:

Change this:

jsx
<div>

To this:

jsx
<div style={{ textAlign: "center" }}>

That's the <div> right after the <span> with the step number (around line 794). It wraps both the green headline and the grey body text, so both will center together.

Its still off. Is it the numbered steps?

6:55 PM

Yes — the step numbers (01, 02) are in a flex row with the text, which pushes everything off-center. The fix is to stack the number above the text instead of beside it. Change the whole return block in the process map to:

jsx
return (
  <div key={i} style={{ marginBottom: "20px", textAlign: "center" }}>
    <span style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "10px", color: "var(--accent)", opacity: 0.5 }}>
      {String(i + 1).padStart(2, "0")}
    </span>
    {headline && (
      <p style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "12px", fontWeight: 700, color: "var(--accent)", marginBottom: "6px", letterSpacing: "0.03em" }}>{headline}</p>
    )}
    <p style={{ fontFamily: "var(--mono)", fontSize: "12px", lineHeight: 1.7, color: "var(--text-secondary)" }}>{body}</p>
  </div>
);

This removes the side-by-side flex layout and stacks the number, headline, and body vertically — all centered.

One last thing. I want to center the toold

7:06 PM

That's the tech stack tags in the Lab cards. Find the LabCard component — look for the div that wraps the tech tags. It'll look like:

jsx
<div style={{ display: "flex", gap: "12px", flexWrap: "wrap" }}>

Add justifyContent: "center" to it:

jsx
<div style={{ display: "flex", gap: "12px", flexWrap: "wrap", justifyContent: "center" }}>

There are two places this appears — once in the LabCard component (the main page cards) and once in the ProjectDetail modal. Update both if you want them centered everywhere.

Find exact line numbers

Line 1323 — that's the LabCard. Add justifyContent: "center" there.

And for the Project Detail modal:

Find the modal tech tags

Line 770 too if you want them centered in the popup as well.

How do i get more flair out of this part. Rid the breaks. Emphasize I AM NOT THE CREATOR

8:26 PM

Two things to fix: the <br><br> tags are rendering as literal text instead of line breaks, and you want "I AM NOT THE CREATOR" to pop visually.

For the data, replace <br><br> with a paragraph separator — I'll update the renderer to handle it. And wrap the emphasized text with double asterisks.

In your outcomes string in the PROJECTS data, change it to something like:

js
outcomes: "The Black Whole is a hybrid generative cinematic system that bridges AI latent space with real time architectural logic. The pipeline begins with an AI training layer and custom tuned models to establish a consistent Afrofuturistic visual language across characters, environments, and fractured states. These generated assets are then ingested into a real time 3D engine, where they are mapped onto dynamic geometry and controlled by a state-driven architecture.\n\nIn this framework, the protagonist's internal condition functions as a system variable, triggering real-time procedural effects—such as spatial distortion, temporal delay, and lighting shifts—that manipulate the AI-generated imagery. By combining the high-fidelity texture of generative synthesis with the responsive logic of a game engine, the project moves beyond static video. The result is an evolving, system-driven environment that can manifest as a cinematic film, an interactive installation, or a live performance framework within the broader **I AM NOT THE CREATOR** ecosystem"

Now let me update the outcomes renderer to support \n\n paragraph breaks and **bold** emphasis:

Ran a command, viewed a file, edited a file

Two changes to make in your local file:

1. The renderer (lines 805–809) — replace with the block above. It now splits on \n\n or <br><br> for paragraph breaks, and wraps anything between **double asterisks** in accent green monospace bold.

2. Your data string — in your outcomes text, use \n\n for paragraph breaks and **I AM NOT THE CREATOR** for the emphasis. For example:

js
outcomes: "The Black Whole is a hybrid generative cinematic system...architecture.\n\nIn this framework, the protagonist's internal condition...the broader **I AM NOT THE CREATOR** ecosystem"

"I AM NOT THE CREATOR" will render in green, bold, monospace — standing out against the surrounding serif text. And the two paragraphs will have proper spacing instead of literal <br> tags showing.

It didn't work. Didnt make a new line or bold.

8:46 PM

The issue is likely that your data string uses literal <br><br> text and the \n\n isn't being interpreted as an actual newline. Let me see your actual outcomes string. Can you paste the exact outcomes text from your project data in the file? Or run this:

bash
grep -A 2 "outcomes:" src/App.jsx

Or whatever file your project data is in. I need to see exactly how the string is written — whether it uses single quotes, backticks, or double quotes, and how the breaks are written.

Most likely fix: change your outcomes string to use backticks (template literal) instead of regular quotes, so \n actually creates newlines:

js
outcomes: `The Black Whole is a hybrid generative cinematic system that bridges AI latent space with real time architectural logic. The pipeline begins with an AI training layer and custom tuned models to establish a consistent Afrofuturistic visual language across characters, environments, and fractured states.

In this framework, the protagonist's internal condition functions as a system variable, triggering real-time procedural effects—such as spatial distortion, temporal delay, and lighting shifts—that manipulate the AI-generated imagery. By combining the high-fidelity texture of generative synthesis with the responsive logic of a game engine, the project moves beyond static video. The result is an evolving, system-driven environment that can manifest as a cinematic film, an interactive installation, or a live performance framework within the broader **I AM NOT THE CREATOR** ecosystem`

Notice the backticks ` instead of quotes ". With backticks, you can just hit Enter for real line breaks — no \n needed. Try that and let me know.

here

8:50 PM

I can see the problem — you're using regular double quotes " so the \n\n and ** are being treated as literal text. Change the opening and closing quotes to backticks:

Change this:

outcomes: "The Black Whole...

To this:

outcomes: `The Black Whole...

And at the end, change:

...ecosystem",

To:

...ecosystem`,

Then replace \n\n with an actual blank line — just hit Enter twice. With backticks you don't need \n\n at all. Keep the **I AM NOT THE CREATOR** as is — that will work once the string is in backticks.
